notes: Hard overlap across document conversion, runtime boundaries, and benchmark planning.
This holdout should expose whether the model over-merges adjacent architecture concepts.

captures.preview: MarkItDown is useful for dropped PDF or DOCX imports, not the live clipboard hot path.
captures.rawText: MarkItDown is useful for dropped PDF or DOCX imports, not the live clipboard hot path. Clipboard rich-text cleanup is still a separate normalization problem.

captures.preview: If we add import adapters, run them inside Rust background jobs, not renderer hooks.
captures.rawText: If we add import adapters, run them inside Rust background jobs, not renderer hooks. The scheduling and retry semantics belong to the compiler runtime.

captures.preview: Need an artifact-level benchmark comparing import adapters on fidelity and cost.
captures.rawText: Need an artifact-level benchmark comparing import adapters on fidelity and cost. We should not pick a conversion library just because the README looks broad.

captures.preview: Ship clipboard eval first and document imports second.
captures.rawText: Ship clipboard eval first and document imports second. The import path is useful, but it should not derail the quality pipeline milestone.

captures.preview: HTML cleanup for copied rich text is still a separate problem.
captures.rawText: HTML cleanup for copied rich text is still a separate problem. A file converter does not replace clipboard-specific normalization.

captures.preview: Python-based import adapters are acceptable if isolated behind async task boundaries.
captures.rawText: Python-based import adapters are acceptable if isolated behind async task boundaries. The runtime contract still belongs to the Rust compiler layer.
